title: Introduction
text:
Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder and a leading cause of dementia worldwide. Aging is the strongest known risk factor, and recent studies have identified multiple senescence-related genetic pathways associated with disease development (Liu et al., 2024).
AD is also one of the leading causes of death in the United States (Figure 1) (Ahmad and Anderson, 2021). Although the categorization of causes of death in this table is debatable due to inconsistent disease hierarchies, the data clearly show that Alzheimer’s disease (AD) alone is responsible for a significant number of deaths in the United States.



Leading causes of death in the United States, 2015–2020. Source: Ahmad and Anderson, JAMA, 2021.


Diagnosis of AD typically involves comprehensive clinical evaluation, including cognitive assessments and neuroimaging techniques such as amyloid PET scans. In 2023, Dhana et al. reported state-level prevalence of Alzheimer’s dementia across the United States based on their four neurocognitive tests and demographic data, identifying Maryland (12.9%), New York (12.7%), Mississippi (12.5%), and Florida (12.5%) as the highest-prevalence states (Figure 2), which are unlikely to have many state-level common characteristics. Interestingly, on the county-level map, there is a region-specific pattern with two big curved lines, which are not fully descried in the original paper.



Prevalence of Alzheimer’s dementia among adults aged ≥65 years across U.S. states and counties (2020). Source: Dhana et al., Alzheimer’s & Dementia, 2023.


Therefore, in this study, I analyze Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS) survey data with U.S. Census data to investigate potential demographic and socioeconomic factors related to the region-specific pattern in the Alzheimer’s dementia prevalence. Specifically, I examine subjective cognitive decline (SCD) from BRFSS survey data as an indicator for early cognitive impairment and compare patterns in high-prevalence states (Maryland, New York, Mississippi, and Florida) to national trends. Additionally, county-level demographic and housing characteristics were analyzed to explore potential socioeconomic AD risk factors to explain the region-specific pattern on the visualized map of AD prevalence. Surprisingly, we observed an unexpected relationship between Alzheimer’s dementia prevalence and the proportion of households receiving “Food Stamps/ Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP)” benefits, suggesting the need for further investigation into socioeconomic determinants of cognitive health. As global life expectancy continues to rise, identifying modifiable risk factors for AD is critical to improving quality of life and alleviating the societal burden of the disease. Ultimately, this ecological study serves as a foundation step in generating robust hypotheses regarding the socioeconomic determinants of cognitive health.

title: An Ecological Study of Prevalence of Alzheimer’s Dementia and Its Potential Risk Factors
text:
This ecological study identified a positive association between Alzheimer’s dementia prevalence and socioeconomic disadvantage, as measured by the proportion of households receiving SNAP benefits. While causal inference is limited due to the aggregated nature of the data, the strong visual correspondence between geographic patterns and the moderate statistical association support this relationship.
Previous studies have reported links between socioeconomic status and dementia risk. For example, Powell et al. (2020) found that individuals residing in the most disadvantaged neighborhoods had significantly higher odds of Alzheimer’s neuropathology. Although that study was limited to two states, the present findings suggest that similar patterns may extend at the national level.
These results highlight the importance of considering broader social and environmental contexts in understanding Alzheimer’s disease risk and emphasize the need for equitable socioeconomic conditions to promote cognitive health.

title: An Ecological Study of Prevalence of Alzheimer’s Dementia and Its Potential Risk Factors
text:

Previous studies have reported links between socioeconomic status and dementia risk. For example, Powell et al. (2020) found that individuals residing in the most disadvantaged neighborhoods had significantly higher odds of Alzheimer’s neuropathology. Although that study was limited to two states, the present findings suggest that similar patterns may extend at the national level.

title: An Ecological Study of Prevalence of Alzheimer’s Dementia and Its Potential Risk Factors
text:
Summary
This study conducted an ecological analysis of Alzheimer’s dementia prevalence using multiple aggregated datasets. While prior work identified distinct geographic patterns at the county level, state-level SCD data did not reveal consistent patterns among the highest-prevalence states. Population-based factors, including total and senior population, were insufficient to explain the observed distribution.
In contrast, socioeconomic indicators—particularly SNAP participation—demonstrated strong spatial similarity and a moderate statistical association with AD prevalence. These findings support the hypothesis that socioeconomic disadvantage may be an important determinant of regional variation in Alzheimer’s disease.

title: An Ecological Study of Prevalence of Alzheimer’s Dementia and Its Potential Risk Factors
text:
Further Discussion

Elevation Effect
Environmental factors were not formally assessed in this study; however, visual comparison suggests a potential similarity between AD prevalence patterns and geographic elevation. Previous studies have reported associations between altitude and dementia-related outcomes, including Alzheimer’s disease mortality (Thielke et al., 2015).
The relationship between altitude and Alzheimer’s disease may be complex, involving both physiological stressors and long-term adaptation mechanisms. While this study does not establish a direct link, the observed spatial similarity warrants further investigation using appropriate environmental and epidemiological data.
In summary, future studies integrating environmental, socioeconomic, and biological factors may provide a more comprehensive understanding of regional variation in Alzheimer’s disease risk.



Prevalence of Alzheimer’s dementia among adults aged ≥65 years across U.S. states and counties (2020). Source: Dhana et al., Alzheimer’s & Dementia, 2023.



(Left) US map with average elevation by state, Source: wikipedia

(Right) US topographic map, Source: wikipedia

title: Results

To assess data completeness in the Alzheimer’s Disease and Healthy Aging dataset (AD dataset), a summary table of missing values was generated for each variable (Table 1). Missing values (NAs) in data_value and confidence interval variables primarily reflect statistical or sampling limitations rather than data errors. Detailed explanations are provided in the data_value_footnote_symbol and data_value_footnote fields.
The first level of stratification contains no missing values, whereas most missingness occurs in the second level, particularly for observations labeled “Overall” within age groups. These missing values are intentionally introduced to avoid duplication across stratification levels. Missing geolocation values correspond to the “United States” category, where geographic coordinates are not required. Overall, the dataset remains suitable for analysis.
Data integrity levels were defined as follows:
Missing (%) = 0 → Complete; Missing (%) < 20 → High Integrity; Missing (%) < 50 → Conditional;

The AD dataset consists of seven modules containing survey questions related to Alzheimer’s disease and its prevention. For each module, the number of observations and the proportion of missing values were calculated (Table 2).
Responses are stratified first by age group and then by gender and race/ethnicity. Observations are reported at the state level for two age groups (50–64 and 65+), with the “Overall” category representing the combined population.

This study focuses on the Cognitive Decline module, which includes measures of subjective cognitive decline (SCD), a potential early indicator of cognitive impairment.
The module contains four key questions:
Q30: Frequency of subjective cognitive decline Q31: Impact on daily activities Q41: Need for assistance Q42: Experience seeking medical help
A detailed mapping of these questions is provided in Table 3.



Table 3: Mapping of Question ID to Topic and Survey Question


Question ID
Summary
Topic
Survey Question




Q30
Subjective cognitive decline (SCD) freq
Subjective cognitive decline or memory loss among older adults
Percentage of older adults who reported subjective cognitive decline or memory loss that is happening more often or is getting worse in the preceding 12 months


Q31
Daily activity impact
Functional difficulties associated with subjective cognitive decline or memory loss among older adults
Percentage of older adults who reported subjective cognitive decline or memory loss that interferes with their ability to engage in social activities or household chores


Q41
Need assistance
Need assistance with day-to-day activities because of subjective cognitive decline or memory loss
Percentage of older adults who reported that as a result of subjective cognitive decline or memory loss that they need assistance with day-to-day activities


Q42
Discussed with doctor
Talked with health care professional about subjective cognitive decline or memory loss
Percentage of older adults with subjective cognitive decline or memory loss who reported talking with a health care professional about it








The proportion of missing data across the four Cognitive Decline questions ranges from approximately 33% to 38% (Figure 1). Q30 exhibits the lowest missingness (33%), while Q41 shows the highest (38%), with Q31 and Q42 at similar intermediate levels.
This relatively consistent level of missingness across questions suggests a systematic pattern likely driven by survey design and stratification structure rather than random data loss. Despite moderate missingness, the data remain comparable across questions and are appropriate for descriptive and comparative analysis.
Importantly, when restricting to overall stratifications, the proportion of missing values in data_value is extremely low (~0.07%), indicating high data completeness and supporting the reliability of analyses based on aggregated groups (Table 4).

title: Results
text:
2. State-Level Analysis

2.1. SCD Comparison of High AD Prevalence States to the U.S. National Level (2015–2022)
To assess whether states with the highest Alzheimer’s dementia (AD) prevalence—Maryland (12.9%), New York (12.7%), Mississippi (12.5%), and Florida (12.5%)—share similar patterns in subjective cognitive decline (SCD), response frequencies from the Cognitive Decline module were analyzed. Stratification by gender and race/ethnicity was excluded, and only aggregated state-level data from 2015 to 2022 were used.
In the jitter plot (Figure 2), these four states were highlighted against the distribution of all states (gray), with the U.S. national aggregate shown in black as a reference. Maryland consistently exhibited lower SCD-related response frequencies than the national average, whereas Mississippi showed higher values across multiple indicators. This suggests that older adults in Mississippi may report greater awareness of cognitive decline and a higher need for assistance, aligning with its elevated AD prevalence. In contrast, Maryland demonstrated an inverse pattern, with lower SCD reporting despite high AD prevalence. Florida and New York showed values similar to the national distribution, indicating no distinct deviation in SCD-related measures.








2.2. Longitudinal SCD Comparison (2015–2022)
To evaluate temporal trends, SCD-related response frequencies were examined longitudinally (Figure 3). The overall patterns remained consistent with cross-sectional findings. Maryland showed persistently lower levels of reported cognitive decline, functional difficulty, and need for assistance compared to the national average across the study period. Conversely, Mississippi consistently exhibited higher levels across these measures, suggesting a sustained burden of perceived cognitive decline.
No substantial differences were observed among the four states in terms of discussions with healthcare providers. Overall, no consistent pattern in SCD measures was identified that could explain the shared high AD prevalence across these states.











2.3. Covid19 Motality Comparision
To estimate the condition of health environment of the high AD prevalence states (highlighted by *), I mapped the COVID19 mortality rates on the national map. Although the causes of high mortality late will vary such as vaccination uptake, public health policies, the demorgraphuc and heath factors such as obeity rates and age, we cannot deny it can be a good indicator total health care environment. However, the four states COVID19 death rates were very representing their different healthcare environment.

title: Results
text:
3. County-Level Analysis of AD Prevalence
The absence of consistent state-level patterns suggests that aggregated statistics may obscure important regional variation. Therefore, county-level AD prevalence data from Dhana et al. (2023) were visualized to explore geographic patterns (Figure 4).
Two prominent curved geographic patterns were observed: one extending along the eastern-to-southern boundary and another spanning north-to-south across the central United States. These patterns suggest the presence of region-specific determinants not captured at the state level.










3.1. Population effect

3.1.1. Total Population
We first hypothesized that these patterns may reflect population distribution. County-level total population data from the U.S. Census were visualized (Figure 5a). Due to skewed population distribution, a log transformation was applied. However, the resulting map did not reproduce the observed AD prevalence patterns.
Population density was then calculated using county area data, and the resulting density map (Figure 5b) showed some linear clustering of populated regions. However, these did not align with the patterns observed in AD prevalence, suggesting that total population alone does not explain the geographic distribution.


















3.1.2. Senior Population (Age ≥65)
Option2 Given that AD prevalence is defined among individuals aged 65 years and older, senior population data were examined. Both the density (Figure 7b) and proportion (Figure 7c) of the senior population were visualized. While these measures provided some regional variation, data coverage was limited, and extreme values in certain counties reduced interpretability. Consequently, no clear alignment with AD prevalence patterns was observed, and further high-resolution data would be required for conclusive analysis.
Option1 Given that Alzheimer’s disease (AD) prevalence is defined among individuals aged 65 years and older, senior population characteristics were further examined. Both the density (Figure 7b) and proportion (Figure 7c) of the senior population were visualized to assess potential geographic patterns. However, consistent with the findings from total population analyses, no distinct or meaningful spatial patterns were observed.

























Removing outlier for better map coloring: Fairfax County, Virginia ( Density: 9610 seniors/km² )



























Table 4: Completeness Summary for Census Population Variables


Variable Name

NA Count
Missing (%)

Senior Population (65+)
3221

3.2. Economic Effect
To evaluate socioeconomic influences, the proportion of households receiving Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) benefits was calculated and mapped at the county level. Strikingly, the spatial distribution of SNAP participation closely mirrored the two curved patterns observed in AD prevalence.
To quantify this relationship, county-level SNAP proportions were plotted against AD prevalence, yielding an \(R^2\) value of 0.305. Although moderate, this association supports a potential relationship between socioeconomic disadvantage and AD prevalence. These findings suggest that poverty-related factors may contribute to regional variation in Alzheimer’s disease burden.

title: About the Data
text:
This page provides a detailed description of the datasets used in this study, along with all preprocessing and data cleaning steps. These procedures ensure transparency and reproducibility of the analysis presented in the main report.


(1) Prevalence of Alzheimer’s Dementia Data (Dhana et al., 2023)

Description:
This dataset originates from the Chicago Health and Aging Project (CHAP), a prospective population-based cohort study designed to evaluate risk factors for Alzheimer’s dementia (AD). Prevalence estimates were derived using neurocognitive assessments and demographic data collected from 10,802 participants through structured in-home interviews conducted in 3-year cycles.

Neurocognitive tests:
Two episodic memory tests, one perceptual speed test, and the Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) assessing general orientation and global cognition.
Demographic data:
Based on NHCS 2020 bridged-race population estimates.

Raw data acquisition:
The dataset was obtained by downloading the supplementary CSV file (NIHMS1888597-supplement-supinfo3.csv).
Data cleaning:
The dataset was processed by separating combined geographic identifiers into county and state variables and extracting numerical values for prevalence and confidence intervals from a single text column using regular expressions. Specifically, the regex pattern ([0-9.]+) \\(([0-9.]+), ([0-9.]+)\\) was used to extract prevalence, lower CI, and upper CI values. Geographic names were standardized by removing suffixes (e.g., “County”) and converting to lowercase to ensure consistent merging with other datasets.


## Data Cleaning of Prevalence of Alzheimer’s Dementia Data 
ad_prevalence <- read.csv("NIHMS1888597-supplement-supinfo3.csv")

# Data Cleaning
ad_prevalence <- ad_prevalence %>%
  # Split county/state 
  separate(Counties, into = c("county", "state"), sep = ", ") %>%
  
  # Extract prevalence + CI using regex
  extract(
    'Prevalence',
    into = c("prevalence", "ci_lower", "ci_upper"),
    regex = "([0-9.]+) \\(([0-9.]+), ([0-9.]+)\\)",
    convert = TRUE
  ) %>%
  
  # Clean names for mapping
  mutate(
    county = str_remove(county, " County"),
    county_lower = tolower(county),
    state_lower = tolower(state)
  )



(2) Alzheimer’s Disease and Healthy Aging Data (BRFSS, 2015–2022)

Description: This dataset, compiled from the Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS), contains state-level survey responses related to health behaviors and conditions among adults aged 50 years and older. It includes seven modules covering health status, lifestyle factors, and cognitive decline.
Cognitive Decline Module: The analysis focused on four questions across four topics related to subjective cognitive decline (SCD), used as an indicator for early cognitive impairment.
Raw data acquisition: Data were retrieved programmatically via the CDC API using paginated requests with limit 5000 and offset 0, combining multiple chunks into a single dataset and saving locally as a CSV file.  View CDC Alzheimer’s Dataset Description.


## Data Acuisition of Alzheimer’s Disease and Healthy Aging Data
library(httr)
library(readr)
library(dplyr)

base_url <- "https://data.cdc.gov/resource/hfr9-rurv.csv"

limit <- 50000
offset <- 0

all_data <- list()

repeat {
  offset_str <- format(offset, scientific = FALSE)   # should be integer formatting (NO scientific notation)
  
  url <- paste0(base_url, "?$limit=", limit, "&$offset=", offset_str)
  cat("Fetching:", url, "\n")
  
  res <- GET(url)
  
  if (status_code(res) != 200) {
    stop("Request failed with status: ", status_code(res))
  }
  
  chunk <- read_csv(content(res, "raw"), show_col_types = FALSE)
  
  if (nrow(chunk) == 0) break
  
  all_data[[length(all_data) + 1]] <- chunk
  
  offset <- as.integer(offset + limit)
}

cdc_data <- bind_rows(all_data)

write_csv(cdc_data, "cdc_dataset.csv")


Data cleaning: Approximately 30% of observations contained missing values, which were retained because they reflect survey design limitations and statistical reliability rather than random absence. A summary table of missing values was generated to assess data completeness (see the table 1 in the Result page). In addition, overall category which was used in this study has only 0.07% of missingness indicating the high completness of the dataset (See table 4 in the Result page).



(3) Demographic and Economic Data (U.S. Census Bureau)

Description: The U.S. Census Bureau provides detailed demographic and housing data through the Decennial Census and American Community Survey (ACS), including population structure, household composition, and socioeconomic indicators.
Datasets used:

Total population (Decennial Census 2020): DECENNIALDHC2020.P1
Population 65 Years and Over (ACS 5-year estimates 2016-2020): ACSST5Y2020.S0103
SNAP (Food Stamps) participation (ACS 5-year estimates 2016-2020): ACSST5Y2020.S2201

Raw data acquisition: Datasets were downloaded as CSV files from the Census data portal after applying filters for geography (county level), year (2020), and relevant topics.
Access Census Data
Data cleaning: Raw Census datasets with multi-row headers and complex column structures were standardized by restructuring column names, selecting relevant variables, and converting population counts to numeric format. Geographic identifiers were harmonized by splitting county and state fields and standardizing text formatting, enabling integration with prevalence and survey datasets. Other datasets from U.S. Census Bureau were processed with the same procedure.


# DATA CLEANING: Senior Population & Percentages
pop_raw <- read_csv("seniorpop5Y2020.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

pop_clean <- pop_raw %>%
  slice(-1) %>%                      # Clean multi-row headers
  select(
    location = NAME,                 # selecting relevant variables
    total_pop = S0103_C01_001E,
    senior_pop = S0103_C02_001E
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    total_pop = as.numeric(total_pop),# converting population counts to numeric format
    senior_pop = as.numeric(senior_pop),
    pct_senior = (senior_pop / total_pop) * 100,
    location = str_trim(location)
  ) %>%
  drop_na(total_pop, senior_pop) %>%
  separate(location, into = c("county", "state"), sep = ", ") %>% #splitting county and state fields
  mutate(
    county = str_remove(county, " County| Parish| Borough| Census Area| Municipality"), #text formatting
    county_lower = tolower(county), # for integration
    state_lower = tolower(state)
  ) %>%
  filter(state_lower != "district of columbia")        



(4) Geographic Data (U.S. Department of Transportation National Transportation Atlas Database (NTAD))

Description:
Geographic boundary data were used to calculate population density for both total and senior populations at the county level. County-level area information was obtained from the U.S. Department of Transportation National Transportation Atlas Database (NTAD), which provides spatial and geographic attributes for U.S. counties. Two area-related variables are included in the dataset: land area (ALAND) and water area (AWATER), both measured in square meters. In this study, only land area (ALAND) was used to calculate population density, as it more accurately reflects habitable space.
Raw data acquisition:
The county-level area dataset (NTAD_Counties.csv) was downloaded from the Department of Transportation’s NTAD database. Population data were obtained separately from the U.S. Census Bureau (Decennial Census 2020).
Data cleaning:
Population and geographic datasets were cleaned and standardized using county-level geographic identifiers (GEOID). In the population dataset, relevant variables were selected, geographic identifiers were reformatted, and population counts were converted to numeric values. Non-county entries were removed to ensure consistency. In the NTAD dataset, land area (ALAND) was extracted and matched to county GEOIDs. These cleaned datasets were subsequently merged to calculate population density as population divided by land area.

Access Counties Data
View Counties Dataset Description

# POPULATION DATA 

pop_raw <- read_csv("totalpop2020.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

pop_clean <- pop_raw %>%
  select(GEO_ID, NAME, `P1_001N`) %>%          # use GEOID
  rename(
    geoid = GEO_ID,
    location = NAME,
    population = `P1_001N`
  ) %>%
  filter(str_detect(location, ",")) %>%
  mutate(
    geoid = str_remove(geoid, "0500000US"),   # Non-county entries were removed
    population = as.numeric(population)       # converted to numeric values
  ) %>%
  drop_na(population)


# 2. NTAD AREA DATA (use GEOID)

ntad_raw <- read_csv("NTAD_Counties.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

ntad_clean <- ntad_raw %>%
  mutate(
    geoid = as.character(GEOID),               # use GEOID
    area_m2 = ALAND
  ) %>%
  select(geoid, area_m2)

title: Cognitive Health in the U.S.
text:
An Ecological Study
of
Prevalence of Alzheimer’s Dementia and Its Potential Risk Factors


BIOS 620 Final Project






Minyoung So


Department of Biostatistics
Universtity of Michigan

title: Methods
text:
This study employed an ecological design integrating multiple publicly available datasets, including county-level Alzheimer’s dementia (AD) prevalence, state-level Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS) survey data, and U.S. Census demographic and socioeconomic data. Data were acquired through direct download (CSV) and API-based retrieval with pagination to ensure completeness. All datasets were cleaned and aligned using standardized geographic identifiers (county/state names and GEOID), and variables were transformed into analyzable formats.
County-level AD prevalence was aggregated to the state level using a population-weighted mean to account for differences in the size of the elderly population:
\[
\text{State Prevalence} = \frac{\sum_{i} p_i \cdot n_i}{\sum_{i} n_i}
\]
where \(p_i\) represents the prevalence in county \(i\) and \(n_i\) denotes the population aged ≥65 years in county \(i\).
Population density was calculated to assess spatial distribution effects using land area:
\[
\text{Population Density} = \frac{\text{Population}}{\text{Land Area (m}^2\text{)}}
\]
and, where appropriate, log-transformed for visualization:
\[
\text{Log Density} = \log(\text{Population Density})
\]
Socioeconomic status was quantified using the proportion of households receiving Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) benefits:
\[
\text{SNAP Proportion} = \frac{\text{Households Receiving SNAP}}{\text{Total Households}}
\]
The association between AD prevalence and socioeconomic factors was assessed using a simple linear model, summarized by the coefficient of determination:
\[
R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}
\]
where \(y_i\) represents observed AD prevalence and \(\hat{y}_i\) represents predicted values from the fitted model.
Subjective cognitive decline (SCD) was analyzed using aggregated BRFSS responses restricted to overall stratifications to minimize missingness and ensure comparability. Descriptive and comparative analyses were conducted across states and over time (2015–2022), and spatial patterns were visualized using county-level maps.
All analyses and visualizations were conducted in R using packages including dplyr, ggplot2, plotly, and kableExtra, ensuring reproducibility and consistency across datasets.
